skill.name: ai-image-generation
skill.description: Generate and edit AI images through the inference.sh CLI using models such as FLUX, Gemini, Grok, Seedream, Reve, and Topaz Upscaler.
skill.summary: Generate, edit, stitch, and upscale AI images through inference.sh model apps.
skill.author: skillssh

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many external command hits and URL hits, but the Ruby backtick and weak cryptography findings are false positives from Markdown content. The skill intentionally uses the infsh CLI to call remote image-generation services, so the real risks are third-party network submission, account cost exposure, and handling of image URLs or prompts.

medium_findings.title: External CLI Sends Prompts and Image Data to Remote Services
medium_findings.description: The skill grants Bash access for infsh commands and documents infsh login plus app run calls. This is the intended function, but prompts, image URLs, and account activity are sent to inference.sh hosted model apps and may incur usage charges.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The Markdown explicitly allows Bash(infsh *) and shows infsh app run commands with prompt and image_url inputs. This confirms remote CLI use, but no malicious exfiltration intent was found.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded External Documentation and Example URLs
low_findings.description: The skill links to inference.sh documentation, a hosted preview image, and placeholder image URLs. These URLs are normal documentation and examples, with no evidence of hidden data exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible links or placeholder inputs in documentation. They do not include secret values, encoded payloads, or suspicious destinations.

low_findings.title: Related Skill Installation Commands Require User Review
low_findings.description: The related skills section contains npx skills add examples. The current skill only allows infsh Bash commands, but users should still review any additional skills before installing them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are documentation for related skills and are outside the declared Bash(infsh *) tool allowance. They can still affect a user environment if copied and executed manually.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Finding Is Not Supported by File Context
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged weak cryptography at the description line, but the line describes image models and use cases. No evidence found of hashing, encryption, or cryptographic code in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is front matter text about AI image generation models. The reviewed file contains no cryptographic operations.

dangerous_patterns.title: Broad infsh Command Allowance
dangerous_patterns.description: The allowed-tools entry permits any infsh subcommand through Bash. This matches the skill purpose, but it increases reliance on the external CLI and its authentication state.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration is explicit and broad for the infsh CLI. The risk is bounded to that CLI rather than arbitrary shell commands.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Model Invocation with User Inputs
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples pass prompts and image URLs to infsh app run. Users should avoid sending confidential prompts, private images, or restricted client assets.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Multiple examples show remote app invocations with prompt and image_url fields. The behavior is intentional and documented, not hidden.

content.value_statement: Creating images across many model providers can require separate tools and settings. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a focused workflow for inference.sh image apps.
actual_capabilities: Runs inference.sh image apps from documented infsh CLI commands.
Generates images from text prompts with FLUX, Gemini, Grok, Seedream, Reve, and related models.
Supports documented image editing, image-to-image, inpainting, stitching, and upscaling workflows.
Lists available image apps through the inference.sh app catalog command.
Provides model selection guidance for speed, cost, quality, text rendering, and LoRA use.
limitations: Requires the inference.sh CLI and an authenticated inference.sh account.
Sends prompts and image URLs to third-party hosted model services.
Output quality, speed, and cost depend on the selected model app.
Does not provide local model execution or offline image generation.

use_cases.title: Create Campaign Visuals
use_cases.description: Generate product-style images, social graphics, and branded concepts from plain language prompts.

use_cases.title: Explore Concept Art
use_cases.description: Test visual directions across cinematic, illustration, and photorealistic models before committing production time.

use_cases.title: Enhance Existing Images
use_cases.description: Use editing, stitching, and upscaling apps to refine source images for presentations or prototypes.

prompt_templates.title: Beginner Text Image
prompt_templates.scenario: Generate a simple image from a short idea.
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate an image of a clean product photo for a ceramic travel mug on a neutral studio background.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Model
prompt_templates.scenario: Ask the assistant to select the best model for a goal.
prompt_templates.prompt: Pick an inference.sh image model for a fast social media concept with readable headline text, then generate the image.

prompt_templates.title: Edit an Existing Image
prompt_templates.scenario: Use a source image and describe the intended change.
prompt_templates.prompt: Edit this image URL so the background becomes a bright modern office while the main product stays unchanged.

prompt_templates.title: Advanced Production Set
prompt_templates.scenario: Create several assets with consistent style and quality constraints.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create three 16:9 campaign visuals with a consistent cinematic style, precise text rendering, and upscale the strongest result.

output_examples.input: Generate a product image for a matte black water bottle on a white studio surface.
output_examples.output: A photorealistic product image is generated with studio lighting, a clean background, and the requested bottle style.

output_examples.input: Upscale an existing image for a presentation slide.
output_examples.output: The selected image is processed through an upscaling app and returned at a higher resolution when the provider completes the job.

output_examples.input: Create a poster image with readable words in the design.
output_examples.output: The skill selects a model suited for text rendering and generates a poster-style image with the requested wording.

best_practices: Use clear prompts that state subject, style, aspect ratio, lighting, and output purpose.
Start with lower-cost or faster models before using premium 4K or cinematic models.
Do not submit confidential images, private client assets, or sensitive prompt details.
anti_patterns: Do not assume every model supports the same fields or image editing features.
Do not paste private image URLs unless they are approved for third-party processing.
Do not run related skill installation commands without reviewing the target skill first.

faq.question: Does this skill generate images locally?
faq.answer: No. It uses the inference.sh CLI to call hosted image model apps.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Do I need an account?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill documentation requires the inference.sh CLI and login before running model apps.

faq.question: Can it edit existing images?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented workflows include image editing, image-to-image, inpainting, stitching, and upscaling apps.

faq.question: Is it safe for confidential images?
faq.answer: Use caution. Prompts and image URLs are sent to external hosted services.

faq.question: Why is the security rating medium?
faq.answer: The skill intentionally runs an external CLI and uses network services, but no malicious behavior was found.
